attributes.stem:
A 73-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with a history of sudden onset weakness of the right arm and leg that has now resolved. She reports a previous similar episode of right-sided weakness, though less severe. Additionally, she has experienced two instances of transient vision loss in the left eye, each lasting several minutes. Her medical history includes hypertension and hypercholesterolaemia. She has a 20 pack-year smoking history.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Neurological examination is unremarkable.

Carotid ultrasound reveals 50-60% stenosis of the left internal carotid artery (ICA) and 40-50% stenosis of the right ICA. These findings are confirmed by CT angiography.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management?
options: ACE inhibitor, high dose statin and life-long aspirin
ACE inhibitor, high dose statin, clopidogrel and life-long aspirin
attributes.explanation:
This patient's history is consistent with multiple transient ischaemic attacks (TIAs), including amaurosis fugax (transient monocular vision loss) and symptoms suggestive of middle cerebral artery (MCA) or anterior cerebral artery (ACA) ischaemia. The left-sided carotid artery stenosis is symptomatic, as evidenced by the ipsilateral amaurosis fugax and contralateral limb weakness.

Aspirin and left carotid endarterectomy is the most appropriate management for this patient. Carotid endarterectomy (CEA) is indicated for symptomatic carotid artery stenosis greater than 50%. In this case, the left ICA stenosis of 50-60% falls within this range. CEA has been shown to substantially reduce the risk of ipsilateral stroke in patients with 50-99% stenosis.

Lifestyle modification only is insufficient for managing symptomatic carotid stenosis of this degree. While lifestyle changes are important, they should be implemented alongside medical therapy and surgical intervention when indicated.

The options of ACE inhibitor, high dose statin and life-long aspirin or ACE inhibitor, high dose statin, clopidogrel and life-long aspirin represent best medical management for carotid stenosis. However, in symptomatic patients with >50% stenosis, these medical therapies alone are not sufficient and should be combined with surgical intervention.

Carotid artery stent insertion is an alternative to CEA, particularly in patients with high surgical risk. However, current evidence suggests that CEA is associated with a lower risk of periprocedural stroke and fewer emboli to the brain during the procedure compared to stenting, especially in older patients.

It is important to appreciate that regardless of the surgical intervention chosen, all patients with carotid stenosis should receive optimal medical management, and this will include anti-platelet therapy (typically aspirin) and high-intensity statin therapy. Blood pressure control with medications such as ACE inhibitors is also crucial in reducing overall cardiovascular risk.
learningPoints: Symptomatic carotid artery stenosis exceeding 50% should be considered for endarterectomy.
Carotid endarterectomy is more effective than carotid artery stenting in reducing stroke risk for symptomatic carotid stenosis.
Best medical management, including anti-platelet therapy and statins, is essential for all patients with carotid artery stenosis.
references: Cardiovascular disease risk stratification [Internet]. eTG Complete. 2017. Available from: https://tgldcdp.tg.org.au/etgcomplete
Stroke and transient ischaemic attack [Internet]. eTG Complete. 2017. Available from: https://tgldcdp.tg.org.au/etgcomplete
Naylor et al. Management of atherosclerotic carotid and vertebral artery disease. 2107 Clinical Practice Guidelines of the European Society for Vascular Surgery.
Bonati LH, Jansen O, de Borst GJ, er al. Management of atherosclerotic extracranial carotid artery stenosis. Lancet Neurol 2022; 21: 273–83.
